title: Does Reading During the Summer Build Reading Skills? Evidence from a Randomized Experiment in 463 Classrooms

instrument.preamble: Project READS is a voluntary scaffolded summer reading program for 2nd and 3rd graders in North Carolina public schools. Students were tested in the spring, assigned within classroom, and then assessed again in the fall on reading comprehension.
instrument.simulatability_note: UNSOUND: sole outcome (ITBS reading comprehension) has no question_text and no scale bounds; LLM has no basis to produce a meaningful numeric response. Needs manual fix before simulation.

treatment_variations.text: Students assigned to the control group received no books during the summer and participated in six mathematics lessons during the spring while the treatment students participated in reading lessons.

Students randomly selected for the treatment group were given six reading-comprehension lessons in the spring focused on engagement with books at home during the summer. Parents were invited to a family literacy event. During the summer, treatment students were mailed 10 books, one per week, matched to baseline reading level and interests, and were encouraged to return a tri-fold with three comprehension questions for each book.

effects.note: Table 4, pooled grade 2 and 3 sample. Outcome is standardized ITBS reading comprehension; paper states scores were normalized to mean 0 and SD 1 in the control group for the full sample.

title: Paying for Kidneys? A Randomized Survey and Choice Experiment

instrument.preamble: Respondents were told about the current kidney procurement system and then asked to evaluate one randomly assigned paid-donor procurement and allocation system as an alternative to the status quo. They made binary referendum-style choices between their assigned alternative system and the current system at five hypothesized transplant-supply levels.
instrument.simulatability_note: The study is a text-based policy survey experiment with explicit treatment descriptions and binary support choices.

treatment_variations.text: Current organ procurement system without compensation to kidney donors.

Kidney donors receive a cash payment of $30,000 from a public agency coordinated by the US Department of Health and Human Services. Kidney allocation follows priority rules based on medical urgency, blood and tissue match, time on the waiting list, age, and distance to the donor.

Kidney donors receive a cash payment of $100,000 from a public agency coordinated by the US Department of Health and Human Services. Kidney allocation follows priority rules based on medical urgency, blood and tissue match, time on the waiting list, age, and distance to the donor.
Kidney donors receive a cash payment of $30,000 directly from the organ recipient. A public agency coordinated by the US Department of Health and Human Services regulates and oversees the process.
Kidney donors receive a cash payment of $100,000 directly from the organ recipient. A public agency coordinated by the US Department of Health and Human Services regulates and oversees the process.

Kidney donors receive noncash compensation valued at $30,000 from a public agency. Donors can choose between tax credits, tuition vouchers, loan repayment, or contributions to a tax-free retirement account. title: Disaggregating Deliberation’s Effects: An Experiment within a Deliberative Poll

instrument.preamble: Participants in a Deliberative Poll around New Haven, Connecticut were randomly assigned to deliberate either airport expansion first and revenue sharing second, or revenue sharing first and airport expansion second. After the first formal on-site session, they completed a midpoint questionnaire (T2), allowing the paper to compare attitudes on issues that had versus had not yet been formally deliberated.
instrument.simulatability_note: Experiment relies heavily on in-person, multi-day, real-time group deliberation and interpersonal discussion among a live sample.

title: The Effect of Effectiveness: Donor Response to Aid Effectiveness in a Direct Mail Fundraising Experiment

instrument.preamble: Freedom from Hunger ran two direct-mail fundraising experiments on recent prior donors in the United States. In wave 1, mail recipients were randomly assigned to a standard emotional appeal or to a very similar mailing that added scientific evidence about program impact. In wave 2, recipients were assigned to a control letter with an emotional appeal or to treatment letters that added impact evidence, with one treatment also explicitly citing Yale-affiliated researchers.
instrument.simulatability_note: Direct-mail field experiment that measures actual monetary charitable donations sent via physical mail over several months.

treatment_variations.text: Recipients received the standard renewal letter plus an emotional insert describing a program participant in Bolivia.

Recipients received the standard renewal letter without the additional story insert. The paper notes that this subgroup was part of the organization's own mailer testing rather than the main research contrast.

Recipients received the same wave 1 emotional insert, except the final paragraph was replaced with language reporting that studies using rigorous scientific methodologies had documented positive impacts of the Freedom from Hunger program.
Recipients received a renewal letter with an emotional appeal about Rita and no research-evidence paragraphs.

Recipients received the wave 2 renewal letter with additional paragraphs describing the positive impact of the program based on scientifically rigorous impact studies, but without naming Yale as the research source.
Recipients received the wave 2 impact-evidence letter, explicitly citing Yale-affiliated researchers as the source of the program-effectiveness statistics.

title: Why Don’t People Insure Late-Life Consumption? A Framing Explanation of the Under-Annuitization Puzzle

instrument.preamble: Adults over age 50 in an Internet survey were randomly assigned to see retirement-income choices framed either as investment decisions or as consumption decisions. The survey also randomized whether any remaining payments after death went to charity or to children. Respondents repeatedly chose whether the life-annuity option or an alternative product represented the better choice.

title: Labor Market Concerns and Support for Immigration

instrument.preamble: Respondents in a broadly representative US online sample were first asked about the labor-market impact of immigration, especially the Mariel boatlift. A random subset then received research evidence stating that the Mariel boatlift had no adverse effects on wages and unemployment in Miami. The study then measured support for immigration using self-reported policy attitudes and petition-signing behavior, with an obfuscated one-week follow-up survey to test persistence.

treatment_variations.text: Respondents were not shown the research evidence about the Mariel boatlift before answering the post-treatment questions on immigration beliefs and policy preferences.

Respondents were shown research evidence stating that the Mariel boatlift had no adverse labor-market impact on wages and unemployment in Miami, along with an explanation that immigrants increased demand for goods and services and thereby created more jobs.

title: Does Promoting One Healthy Behavior Detract from Others? Evidence from a Field Experiment

instrument.preamble: Participants aged 18 to 35 in the United States downloaded one smartphone app for meditation and one for meal logging, completed baseline surveys, and were randomized into one of five interventions for four weeks. The study measured daily meditation and meal logging from app data during treatment and for four additional weeks after treatment.
instrument.simulatability_note: Real-world field experiment tracking participant engagement in physical behaviors (meditation and meal logging) over 4 weeks via smartphone apps.

treatment_variations.text: Participants received no intervention.
Participants received twice-daily text messages promoting meditation, including one reminder and one informational message each day.
Participants received twice-daily text messages promoting nutritional self-monitoring via meal logging, including one reminder and one informational message each day.
Participants received the full set of meditation messages and meal-logging messages, resulting in four daily messages in total.
Participants earned green or red lottery tickets based on whether they logged meals each day; a drawn green ticket was worth a $10 Amazon gift certificate.

title: Nudging Demand for Academic Support Services: Experimental and Structural Evidence from Higher Education

instrument.preamble: More than 2,100 students at a large public university were randomized into a multifactor advertising experiment about academic support services. The control group received no outreach, while treatment messages varied by service advertised, communication medium, incentive, and timing. The three services were academic coaching, peer tutoring, and extra practice problems.
instrument.simulatability_note: Field experiment tracking real students' uptake of university tutoring and academic support services.

treatment_variations.text: Students assigned to control received no encouragement messages about academic support services during the term.

Students were assigned advertising messages about one of the academic support services. Across message arms, the content promoted coaching, tutoring, or extra practice problems, with additional randomization over incentive attachment and timing.
Students received the advertising intervention via email rather than no outreach. The paper reports email-specific effects within the subsample that provided a mobile phone number.
Students were assigned messages advertising extra practice problems, the treatment dimension that generated the clearest behavioral response in the paper.

title: Sharing responsibility for the good

instrument.preamble: Participants in a preregistered online experiment made decisions about how much of a $2.50 endowment they were willing to pay so that a child in need would receive full measles vaccination through UNICEF. The main comparison was between an individual active-decision condition and a bilateral condition in which vaccination occurred only if both matched participants stated willingness to pay at or above a drawn price. A third individual-passive condition collected hypothetical choices from passive partners.

treatment_variations.text: Participants individually chose the maximum amount of their $2.50 endowment they were willing to pay for a child's measles vaccination using a Becker-DeGroot-Marschak mechanism. Their decision was payoff-relevant.
Participants were informed about the individual active decision and then stated the willingness to pay they would choose in the active role, but their choice had no monetary consequences.
Participants made the same willingness-to-pay decision, but the vaccination donation was made only if both matched participants stated willingness to pay at or above the randomly drawn price.

title: Creating Exercise Habits Using Incentives: The Trade-off Between Flexibility and Routinization

instrument.preamble: Google employees who wanted to exercise more regularly chose a daily two-hour workout window and were then randomized into a no-incentive control group, flexible incentive groups that paid for any qualifying weekday gym visit, or routine incentive groups that paid only for qualifying gym visits started within the chosen window.
instrument.simulatability_note: Field experiment requiring participants to physically visit a corporate gym, tracked via ID badge swipes.

title: People Judge Discrimination Against Women More Harshly Than Discrimination Against Men – Does Statistical Fairness Discrimination Explain Why?

instrument.preamble: Respondents in a representative US Qualtrics sample and a larger US MTurk sample were shown hiring scenarios in which a manager hired the less qualified applicant. instrument.preamble: US respondents recruited on Prolific completed a paired-profile conjoint experiment about a city-government community activity worker position. title: Bridging America’s Divide on Abortion, Guns and Immigration: An Experimental Study

instrument.preamble: A nationally representative US sample first stated its views on immigration, gun laws, and abortion, then listened to recordings from people with opposite policy views. Before listening, respondents were randomized to either a control condition, a treatment emphasizing shared behavioral etiquette rules, or a treatment emphasizing shared human-rights values with the speaker.
instrument.simulatability_note: CANNOT SIMULATE: core stimulus is an audio recording; outcome questions ask 'after hearing the recording, how much did your view change?' — unanswerable without audio content. Text framing alone does not carry the persuasive load.

treatment_variations.text: You are given the opportunity to listen to an audio recording from someone who holds the opposite view on a policy issue, without any additional common-ground framing.
Before listening to the opposing view, you are explicitly reminded that you and the speaker share the same basic behavioral etiquette rules.
Before listening to the opposing view, you are explicitly reminded that you and the speaker share the same basic human-rights values.

title: COVID-19 Information Treatments Shift Related Policy Preferences and Plans for Consumer Behavior

instrument.preamble: A nationally representative US sample was randomly assigned to one of nine brief text-based information segments about COVID-19, school safety, masks, travel, hospitalizations, or unemployment. The study then measured binary policy-preference, intended-behavior, and belief outcomes. The paper uses the record-high-case segment as its main reference group.
instrument.simulatability_note: SIMULATABLE: purely text-based COVID information treatments; outcomes are binary policy preferences. Partially extracted (4 of 9 arms, 3 of 15 outcomes) but internally complete.

treatment_variations.text: Respondents saw information that the American Academy of Pediatrics strongly advocated starting policy for the coming school year with the goal of students being physically present in school because the importance of in-person learning is well documented and closures harmed children.
Respondents saw information emphasizing the record high number of new confirmed coronavirus cases in the United States.
Respondents saw information that roughly 77,000 Americans were currently hospitalized with coronavirus, exceeding the previous peak of 60,000 hospitalizations in April.
Respondents saw economic information from the US Bureau of Labor Statistics reporting an unemployment rate of 6.9 percent in October.

title: A Word is Worth A Thousand Words: Biased Beliefs about Political Language Use

instrument.preamble: US MTurk respondents answered a true-false question about whether a Democratic Socialist country could have only one brand of pizza, then predicted what fractions of politically Left, Moderate, and Right respondents would answer similarly. One randomized group additionally faced a financial incentive: the top 5 percent of respondents by prediction accuracy received a 10-dollar bonus.
instrument.simulatability_note: SIMULATABLE: arms vary meaningfully by respondent ideology (left/moderate/right) and incentive condition — LLM can role-play each perspective to predict others' beliefs.

Forecaster identifies as politically Right and makes predictions under the financial-incentive treatment, where the most accurate respondents can earn a bonus.

title: Do people trust humans more than ChatGPT?

instrument.preamble: Participants on Prolific read one short paragraph about the history of statistics that was either human-written or generated by ChatGPT. Some participants were explicitly informed of the paragraph's authorship, while others were not. Participants then judged whether the paragraph contained factual errors, could purchase a costly fact-check, and reported whether they believed the paragraph had been written by a human or by AI.
instrument.simulatability_note: A second manual pass confirmed the exact treatment-cell counts in Table 2 and the exact high-trust proportion for the informed arms. The uninformed comparisons are reported through Figure 2b, belief-based subgroup comparisons, and ordered-logit coefficients rather than exact arm-level high-trust shares by actual treatment, so only the informed comparison is coded numerically.

treatment_variations.text: Participants were explicitly told that the paragraph they saw was written by a human.
Participants were explicitly told that the paragraph they saw was written by AI/ChatGPT.
Participants read a human-written paragraph but were not told the author identity in advance.
Participants read an AI-written paragraph but were not told the author identity in advance.

outcome_questions.outcome_name: High trust that the paragraph had no factual errors
outcome_questions.question_text: Did the participant choose the response indicating that the paragraph contained no factual errors, without purchasing a fact-check?
outcome_questions.response_instruction: Reply with exactly one of: YES or NO.

effects.outcome_name: High trust that the paragraph had no factual errors
effects.note: Table 2 and the discussion of Figure 2a/Table 4. In both informed treatments, about 20 percent of participants exhibit high trust in the paragraph's factual accuracy, and the paper reports no meaningful informed-arm difference.
